coredata.dc:title: Dopamine assay based on an aggregation-induced reversed inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Feng-e
Gui, Chuang
Wen, Wei
Bao, Ting
Zhang, Xiuhua
Wang, Shengfu
coredata.dc:description: We describe a fluorescent dopamine assay that is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The green fluorescence of GQDs is remarkably inhibited in the presence of citrate-stabilized AuNPs via IFE. Upon the addition of dopamine (DA), aggregation of the AuNPs occurs which is associated with a color change from red to blue. The IFE can no longer occur and the fluorescence of GQDs is recovered. Under the optimum conditions, a linear correlation exists between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DA in the range from 20nM to 200nM with a detection limit of 15nM (at 3σ/s). The assay is rapid, inexpensive and highly sensitive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Inner filter effect
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of citrate-stabilized AuNPs
Morphological characteristics of AuNPs and GQDs
Effects of AuNPs on the fluorescence of GQDs
IFE-based AuNPs-GQDs emission response to DA concentration
Detection of DA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405035 , 21475032 ), the Natural Science Fund for Creative Research Groups of Hubei Province of China (No. 2014CFA015 ), and the Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology (No. KL2013M09).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of AuNPs in the absence of dopamine (upper left inset: size distribution of AuNPs in the absence of dopamine measured by DLS; bottom right inset: zeta potential of AuNPs at pH=7.0). (B) TEM image of AuNPs in the presence of 50µM dopamine (upper left inset: size distribution of AuNPs in the presence of 50µM measured by DLS). (C) TEM image of GQDs (bottom right inset: zeta potential of GQDs at pH=7.0).

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of increasing concentrations of AuNPs. The concentration of AuNPs in samples (a)–(h) was 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4nM respectively. The concentration of GQDs was 0.20mgmL−1, dopamine was 100nM.

(A) Absorption spectrum: AuNPs (a), AuNPs in the presence of 100nM dopamine (b), and GQDs (c). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra: GQDs (a), GQDs in the presence of 100nM dopamine (b), GQDs and AuNPs in the presence of 100nM dopamine (c), GQDs and AuNPs (d).

(A) Absorption ratio (A670/A520) of the DA-AuNPs at different reaction time in the presence of 100nM dopamine. (B) The fluorescence enhanced efficiency of GQDs at different concentration of GQDs. AuNPs: 6.5nM; dopamine: 100nM, pH: 7.0. (C) The fluorescence enhanced efficiency of GQDs at different concentration of AuNPs. GQDs: 0.20mgmL−1; dopamine: 100nM; pH: 7.0. (D) The effect of pH on the fluorescence enhanced efficiency. AuNPs: 6.5nM; GQDs: 0.20mgmL−1; dopamine: 100nM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the AuNPs-GQDs system in the presence of different concentrations of dopamine (λexc=468nm). From a to k: 0, 20, 40, 60, 100,120, 140, 160, 180, 200nM, AuNPs: 6.5nM; GQDs: 0.20mgmL−1; pH: 7.0. (B) The fluorescence enhanced efficiency as a function of the concentration of dopamine.
Specificity of AuNPs-GQDs system toward dopamine.
Schematic illustration of the dopamine assay based on an aggregation-induced reversed inner filter effect of AuNPs on the fluorescence of GQDs.
Analytical performances of recently developed assays for the determination of dopamine.
Analytical results for DA in the serum sample.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The serum sample was diluted 50 times.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average of three detections with this method.

head.ce:title: Dopamine assay based on an aggregation-induced reversed inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry of Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry of Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We describe a fluorescent dopamine assay that is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The green fluorescence of GQDs is remarkably inhibited in the presence of citrate-stabilized AuNPs via IFE. Upon the addition of dopamine (DA), aggregation of the AuNPs occurs which is associated with a color change from red to blue. The IFE can no longer occur and the fluorescence of GQDs is recovered. Under the optimum conditions, a linear correlation exists between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DA in the range from 20nM to 200nM with a detection limit of 15nM (at 3σ/s). The assay is rapid, inexpensive and highly sensitive.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a biogenic catecholamine formed by decarboxylation of 3, 4-dihydroxyphenylalanine. It is well known as one of the most important catecholamine neurotransmitters in the mammalian central nervous system [1,2], which plays an essential role in the function of human metabolism, cardiovascular, central nervous, renal, and hormonal systems [3]. The abnormalities in DA levels are associated with several disease states such as schizophrenia [4], Parkinson's [5] and Alzheimer's diseases [6]. Therefore, a sensitive and selective detection of DA is of great importance in the clinical diagnosis of neurological diseases. Substantial efforts have been developed for the quantitative determination of DA, including electrochemistry [7,8], photoelectrochemistry [9], colorimetry [10], high-performance liquid chromatography [11], capillary electrophoresis [12] and fluorescent sensor [13]. However, most of the above methods need expensive equipments, complicated procedures or have a poor reproducibility. Compared with the above approaches, fluorescence spectroscopy for DA analysis has attracted considerable attention because of its excellent performance, including time saving, fast response, cost effectiveness, good stability and reproducibility. Therefore, a new method for DA detection was developed based on the fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

In the past decades, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) have received extensive interest for their unique optical performances [14,15]. However, the intrinsic defects of those SQDs limit their widespread application, such as complex synthesis procedures, heavy metals potential toxicity and environmental hazards [16]. GQDs show potential for replacing traditional SQDs in some aspects [17]. GQDs are single atom-thick graphene sheets with the small size less than 10nm. It is a biocompatible material with very low cytotoxicity owing to its ultra-small size and high oxygen content, and it has no material accumulation in main organs [18]. Low concentrations of GQD were relatively non-toxic. However, it disrupts the progression of embryonic development with the concentrations over 50μg/mL [19]. GQDs have many advantages, such as high fluorescent activity, robust chemical inertness with long fluorescence lifetime and excellent photostability [20–24]. Compared with other carbon-based nanomaterials, GQDs have higher quantum yield [25,26]. GQDs have been proved to be potential candidates in photovoltaic devices, sensing and imaging fields [27]. In the past two decades, some fluorescent competitive assays have been used for detection of ions [28], small molecule [29] and protein [30], via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In these reports, QDs acted as a desirable fluorescent donor and AuNPs as an excellent receiver to substitute traditional organic dyes and quenchers. However, in order to ensure the valid interaction, the conformation of the FRET progress needs a particular distance between AuNPs and QDs. Thus, a sophisticated surface-chemical modification of the adoptive AuNPs or QDs is needed to establish a FRET-based indicator, which markedly makes the detecting process very time-consuming, costly and cumbersome, which further weakens the practicality.

Herein, we demonstrated a sensitive and reliable DA indicating system based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of citrate-stabilized AuNPs (absorber) on the fluorescence of GQDs (fluorophore), which is different from the above AuNPs/QDs-based FRET detecting system. It has been reported that the AuNPs/QDs-based IFE system were used to detecting melamine [31,32]. However, they used SQDs as fluorophores which were complicated operation and environmental hazards compared with GQDs. The principle of our method is illustrated in Scheme 1. The method was further successfully applied to detect DA in real samples with satisfactory results. It is expected that the application of our method as sensing platform can be extended to other biological samples and environmental systems.

Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (C6H11Na3O7·2H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (www.sigma-aldrich.com, USA). Dopamine hydrochloride (C8H11NO2·HCl) was purchased from Aladdin (http://aladdin-e.bioon.com.cn, China). The GQDs (XF075) were synthesized and purified by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd (http://www.xfnano.com, Nanjing, China). All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade. DA solution was prepared by dissolving some DA with ultrapure water as the stock solution (1M), and the working solution of DA was prepared by serial dilution of the stock solution. To get the GQDs solution, the GQDs powders were first dissolved in ultrapure water to 1mgmL−1 solution. Then the solution was put into ultrasonic cleaners for 90min to make it dispersed.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-3300 spectrophotometer (Japan) from Shimadzu company at a room temperature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were made on Tecnai G20 (FEI, USA). Ultrasonic cleaners (Branson2000, USA) and Delta 320pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland) were used. The size distribution and zeta potentials were estimated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments). All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.25MΏcm) produced by Aquapro ultra-pure water system (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of citrate-stabilized AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal solution of citrate-stabilized AuNPs was prepared using Frens’ method described in a previous report [33]. All glasswares used in the experiment were soaked in aqua regia and rinsed thoroughly with water and oven-dried prior to use. Briefly, in a 250mL round-bottom flask equipped with a condenser, 100mL of 1mM HAuCl4 was heated to a rolling boil with vigorous stirring. Then, rapid addition of 10mL of 38.8mM sodium citrate to the vortex of the solution resulted in a color change from pale yellow to wine red and continued for boiling for 15min. After that, heating the solution was stopped, but the solution was stirred until it cooled down to room temperature. Then, the prepared colloid AuNPs were stored in brown glass bottles at 4°C. The molar extinction coefficient of AuNPs at 520nm for 13nm spherical AuNPs is about 2.7×108 M –1 cm−1, thus the concentration of the obtained AuNPs was calculated to be 13nM according to Lambert Beer's law [34]. The final AuNPs had an average diameter of approximately 13nm, which was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1A.

The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with the excitation of 468nm, and the slit widths of excitation and emission are 5nm in all tests. Typically, 200µL AuNPs (13.0nM), 200µL 10mM (pH=7.0) phosphate buffer and 10µL various amount of DA solution were mixed in a series of 1.5mL centrifuge tube. After stabilizing for 10min, 100µL 0.20mgmL−1 GQDs were introduced to the tube and further incubated for 5min. The fluorescence intensity (at 564nm) of the mixture solutions were recorded with an excitation at 468nm. The procedures for all the optimization and control experiments were the same as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characteristics of AuNPs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A displayed the typical TEM images of the AuNPs in the absence of DA. It can be seen that AuNPs were spherical in shape, monodisperse, and fairly uniform in size (about 13nm). The dispersed 13nm AuNPs (pH=7.0) displayed the zeta potential of −10.4mV (the bottom right inset of Fig. 1A), which proved the AuNPs was negative charged. In the presence of DA (50µM), the AuNPs come into aggregation (Fig. 1B) which indicated the interaction between DA and AuNPs. Fig. 1C showed TEM images of GQDs. Their diameters were mainly distributed in the range of 5nm, suggesting that the GQDs solutions were uniformly arranged. The zeta potential of the GQDs (pH=7.0) was measured to be −24.6mV (the bottom right inset of Fig. 1C), due to the –COOH group and –OH group on the surface of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of AuNPs on the fluorescence of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The scheme of this paper was based on the IFE of AuNPs on the fluorescence of GQDs. The mechanism of IFE referred to the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophore absorbed by absorber in the detection system. Specifically, the fluorescence emission of the fluorophore would be tuned by the mutative absorption of the absorber when the absorption spectrum of the absorber has an overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of the fluorophore. Thus the fluorescence analysis of the analyte would be feasible because the absorption response of the indicator to analyte was precisely and effectively converted to the fluorescence signal. Since the changes in the absorbance of the absorber transform into the fluorescence changes of the fluorophore exponentially, an enhanced sensitivity for the detection of the analyte was achieved with respect to the absorbance of the absorber alone [35].

As shown in Fig. 2, the emission intensity of GQDs gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of AuNPs. Due to the electrostatic repulsive force, there was no efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer process occurred between the negative charged AuNPs and the negative charged GQDs. Therefore, the observed fluorescence decrease should be mainly attributed to the IFE of AuNPs on the fluorescence of GQDs. With increasing concentration of AuNPs, the absorbance of the absorber increased, which effectively weakened the emission light of GQDs. As a result, the emission intensity of GQDs gradually decreased. This approach had the advantages of no demand of specific modification of AuNPs and GQDs, leading to the detecting process became more flexible and simple by only utilizing the two materials. Therefore, a sensitive and simple approach was constructed for DA detection via IFE between GQDs and AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFE-based AuNPs-GQDs emission response to DA concentration
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs were aggregated in the presence of DA because amine group of DA tends to readily adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs, and then hydrogen bonding interactions of hydroxy group (-OH) between neighboring DA caused the cross-linking aggregation of AuNPs, which contributed to the recovery of fluorescence emission of GQDs effectively [36]. It was reasonable to expect that the IFE of AuNPs on GQDs would be affected by DA, which aggregated the AuNPs and modulated the IFE process (Scheme 1), and the IFE-based GQDs emission would respond to DA concentration.

The citrate-stabilized AuNPs were wine red and well dispersed (the upper left inset of Fig. 1A), when the DA was added, AuNPs turned blue and aggregated (the upper left inset of Fig. 1B) and the absorption at 520nm diminish (Scheme 1A). Amine group of DA tended to readily adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs via the ligand exchange with citrate ions. H-bonding interactions between neighboring DA's hydroxy group (-OH) induced AuNPs aggregated which rapidly and strongly decreased the stability of the AuNPs [36]. As shown in Scheme 1(B), due to the strong absorption of AuNPs at 520nm, the fluorescence of GQDs was obviously decreased in the presence of citrate-stabilized AuNPs via IFE. However, when different concentrations of DA were added to AuNPs, the fluorescences of GQDs were variously recovered. The fluorescence recovery efficiency exhibited a good linear dependence on DA concentration in a wide range with high sensitivity. Generally, an adequate collaboration of absorber/fluorophore was very difficult to find and establish, since an effective IFE would occur only if a broad overlap existed between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation and/or emission banded of the fluorophore. AuNPs can be utilized as extraordinarily effective absorber in IFE-based fluorescence assay, because of their excellent characteristics, such as extremely large extinction coefficient and flexibly tuned absorption spectrum. At the same time, GQDs can be used as fluorophore in IFE-based fluorescence assay because of their good fluorescence (FL) activity.

To verify this expected principle for DA detection, the effects of DA on the absorption and fluorescence spectra of AuNPs-GQDs were shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3A, dopamine-induced aggregation of AuNPs was accordant with the spectra changes (curve a and curve b). It showed there was no obvious UV–vis absorption of GQDs (curve c). Seen from Fig. 3B, the fluorescence spectra of GQDs (curve a) were identical to those of the mixture of DA and GQDs (curve b), which demonstrated that there was no molecular interaction between DA and GQDs. When GQDs were mixed with AuNPs, the fluorescence emission was decreased (curve d) because of the IFE of AuNPs. However, the IFE-decreased fluorescence of GQDs was recovered obviously with the presence of DA (curve c). No notable change in the shape of the emission spectra of GQDs appeared in the presence of AuNPs and DA, demonstrating that the recovered emission was still distributed by GQDs rather than any other possible newly-formed emission centers. DA does not enhance the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs in the absence of AuNPs. Thus, the interaction between DA and AuNPs contributed to the observed fluorescence recovery which affected the emission of the GQDs. DA causes a decrease in the absorbance of the plasmon absorption band of AuNPs at 520nm which weakened the IFE of AuNPs on GQDs and then lead to the recovery of the IFE-decreased fluorescence emission of GQDs. Due to the recover effect of DA to the fluorescence of AuNPs-GQDs, developing a novel, simple and sensitive IFE-based fluorescence indicator for rapid determination of DA in real sample was necessary to be further studied.

The parameters including incubation time, GQDs concentration, AuNPs concentration and media pH were optimized for the AuNPs-GQDs system. According to previous reports [32], the fluorescence of GQDs was obviously decreased due to the IFE of AuNPs in 1min and almost remained unchanged in the next 9min. To ensure the consistency throughout the experiment and obtain stable signal, 5min was chosen as the optimum incubation time.

We optimized the reaction time of DA-AuNPs binding and the fluorescence of the reaction between GQDs and AuNPs. The relationship between DA-AuNPs binding time and absorption spectra was investigated by recording the spectra every 30s after addition of dopamine into AuNPs solution. The results showed that the aggregation of AuNPs completed within 10min and then kept stable ( Fig. 4A). So, 10min was chosen as the reaction time of DA-AuNPs.

Due to the concentration of GQDs played an important role in the IFE of AuNPs on GQDs, we also optimized the concentration of GQDs. We can draw the conclusion from fluorescence enhanced efficiency, which was defined as (F−F0)/F0. The fluorescence enhanced efficiency reached the maximum value when the concentration reached to 0.20mgmL− 1. After that, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency gradually decreased as shown in Fig. 4B. Therefore, we chose 0.20mgmL−1 GQDs as the best concentration of GQDs.

Besides, the concentration of AuNPs in the system was found to greatly affect the fluorescence response toward DA. As shown in Fig. 4C, it was obvious that the fluorescence enhanced efficiency gets stronger gradually with increasing the concentration of AuNPs until the concentration reached 6.5nM. When the concentration of AuNPs exceeds 6.5nM, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency changes in the opposite trend. This was because the presence of excess amount of AuNPs would not be protected by DA molecules sufficiently. Therefore, GQDs would have more opportunities to be closer to the AuNPs, inducing fluorescence intensity weaken more thoroughly. According to the above analysis, 6.5nM of AuNPs was chosen as the optimum AuNPs concentration.

In addition, the fluorescent intensity of GQDs was closely related to the pH value of media. As reported in the literature [36], the pH value of media affects the interaction of AuNPs with small molecules. We investigated the pH effects from 6.6 to 7.6 (Fig. 4D), while keeping the DA concentration unchanged. Obviously, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency (F−F0)/F0 was obtained at pH 7.0. Moreover, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency (F−F0)/F0 was quite low in strong acidic solution (pH<3.0), where DA and AuNPs were protonic which was unfavorable for the hydrogen-bonding interactions. On the other hand, the AuNPs were unstable in strong basic media, and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs remained stable at this pH value of media. Thus, pH 7.0 was chosen in the following experiments.

Under the above optimum conditions, the analytical parameters of the present method for DA detection were then investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescent recovery efficiency was proportional to the concentration of dopamine. The calibration graph was linear over the range of from 20nM to 200nM (R2=0.99) and the calibration equation is expressed as following: (F−F0)/F0=0.0090c−0.1404 (c: nM). The limit of detection (3σ/s) is 15nM, where σ represents the standard deviation of ten blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with the existing methods including electrochemistry [37], electrospinning [38] and colorimetry [39], a relatively competitive LOD was achieved. We also listed parts of the recently developed immunoassays toward dopamine for comparison, as shown in Table 1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 6.4% which indicated that the fluorescence response of AuNPs-GQDs toward dopamine was provided with high reproducibility.

The selectivity of this method was investigated by comparing the fluorescence recovery with respect to functional and structural interfering analogues such as 5-hydroxyindole acetic acid (5-HIAA) and epinephrine (EP), cyanuric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), purines, uric acid (UA). As shown in Fig. 6, selective fluorescence recovery by DA (0.1 μM) over other interferents were observed in this assay. There was almost no apparent enhancement of fluorescence intensity was detected for 5-HIAA (1μM), EP (1μM), CA (1μM), AA (1μM), purines (1μM), UA (1μM), phenethylamine (1μM), phenol (1μM), catechol (1μM). Thus, the AuNPs-GQDs detecting system herein show a good selective fluorescence response toward DA. The amine group of DA could form the complex of [AuCl(NH2R)], which led to the DA absorbed on the AuNPs, then hydrogen bonding interactions of hydroxy group (–OH) between neighboring DA caused the cross-linking aggregation of AuNPs and contributed to the recovery of fluorescence emission of GQDs effectively, ensuring the high selectivity of the system [36,40]. What is more, the AuNPs and GQDs have high stability, and the mixture of them also showed high stability, which ensured the operational stability of the proposed system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the reliability of the method to specifically detect DA in real samples, we tested spiked serum samples with different DA concentration. In order to diminish false positive contributions from the matrix, the real samples had been diluted 50 times before each experiment. Table 2 showed the analysis results of DA in the spiked serum samples. The recoveries of standard addition were measured between95–100.1%, indicating that the method was highly accurate and reproducible for rapid detection of DA in the spiked serum samples.

In summary, a sensitive turn-on fluorescent detection method for dopamine was developed using the inner filter effect of AuNPs on GQDs. Upon addition of dopamine to AuNPs, the fluorescence emission of GQDs was efficiently recovered due to dopamine induced aggregation of AuNPs. Under.

the optimum conditions, dopamine was detected with high sensitivity and low detection limit of 15nM. The whole procedure including sample pre-treatment was easy to operate and was accomplished within 30min, which save detection time greatly. And it appeared to be a potential for rapid screening of dopamine contamination in raw sample.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405035, 21475032), the Natural Science Fund for Creative Research Groups of Hubei Province of China (No. 2014CFA015), and the Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology (No. KL2013M09).
